examples.title: Basic Message Passing Between Nodes
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the fundamental message passing mechanism used for communication and coordination in distributed systems.
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the core communication mechanism in distributed systems - message passing. Nodes do not share memory, so they must communicate by sending serialized messages over the network. The send_message procedure handles serialization, routing, and transmission, while receive_message handles reception and deserialization.
examples.timeComplexity: O(m) where m is message size

examples.title: Distributed Resource Access with Transparency
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how location transparency is achieved - users access resources without knowing their physical location in the distributed system.
examples.explanation: This demonstrates location transparency - users call access_resource() without knowing where the resource is physically stored. The RESOURCE_DIRECTORY acts as middleware that hides the actual node location from the user, providing a unified namespace.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for directory lookup + O(n) for network access where n is distance
examples.spaceComplexity: O(d) where d is number of entries in directory

examples.title: Handling Partial Failure in Distributed System
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how a distributed system handles partial failures - a key challenge where some nodes may fail while others continue operating.
examples.code:
FUNCTION execute_distributed_task(task, node_list)
 BEGIN
 results = {}
 failures = {}
 
 // Try to execute on each node
 FOR node IN node_list DO
 BEGIN
 TRY
 // Attempt to contact node
 response = node.execute(task)
 results[node.id] = response
 
 CATCH node_failure
 // Handle partial failure
 failures[node.id] = "Node unreachable"
 CONTINUE // Continue with other nodes
 ENDTRY
 END
 
 // Aggregate results despite partial failures
 IF results IS NOT EMPTY THEN
 RETURN aggregate(results)
 ELSE
 RETURN error: "All nodes failed"
 ENDIF
 END

// Example: Processing continues even if some nodes fail
FUNCTION process_with_redundancy(data, replicas)
 BEGIN
 success_count = 0
 
 FOR replica IN replicas DO
 IF is_node_alive(replica) THEN
 replica.store(data)
 success_count = success_count + 1
 ENDIF
 ENDFOR
 
 // System continues if quorum is met (CAP theorem)
 IF success_count >= quorum_threshold THEN
 RETURN success
 ELSE
 RETURN error: "Insufficient replicas available"
 ENDIF
 END
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates partial failure handling, a fundamental challenge in distributed systems. Unlike centralized systems where failure is total, distributed systems can experience failures in only some nodes. The code shows how to continue operation with available nodes and make decisions based on quorum/partial success, aligning with CAP theorem principles.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes contacted
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) where f is number of failed nodes tracked
